problem_summary: 2011 MCM Problem C (interdisciplinary): assess whether widespread adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) is feasible and beneficial. The team had to (1) model the environmental, economic, and health impacts of large-scale EV use, (2) identify the key factors governments and manufacturers should weigh, (3) estimate the oil/fossil fuel the world would save, and (4) recommend the amount and type of electricity generation needed to support optimal EV use. This paper attacks all four sub-tasks with a separate, named model for each, using a gasoline RAV4 vs. all-electric RAV4 EV as the running comparison case and China private-car data for the adoption forecast.
models_used: Life Cycle Cost (LCC) model — consumer-perspective total cost of ownership decomposed into acquisition cost C_pi, maintenance cost C_mi (with a battery-replacement sub-term b = (L2/lb)(p_b+c) and a charging-cost term o2 = L2·p_e·k2/η), reclamation income R_i, and environmental/tax cost C_ei; LCC_i = C_pi + C_mi − R_i + C_ei
Sensitivity analysis — perturb acquisition cost, oil/electricity price, battery-pack price, and mileage each by ±5% and rank the resulting %-change in LCC to find the dominant cost driver

Gaussian Plume dispersion model — point-source ground-level emission concentration C(x,y,z)=q/(πuσyσz)·exp(−0.5(y²/σy²+z²/σz²)); simulated in MATLAB for gasoline CV vs. thermal-powered EV vs. natural-gas EV to compare spatial spread and concentration of air pollutants

AP/GHG emission-split + normalized weighting coefficients — emissions partitioned into Air Pollution (CO, SOx, NO, HC) and Greenhouse Gas (CO2, CH4); normalized weights 6.11 / 3.86 / 1 for CV / thermal-EV / natural-gas-EV used to drive the dispersion visualization
Scenario-based emission projection — three penetration scenarios (20/50/80% EV) with S_AP(i)=CV_AP·p_i·n + EV_AP·q_i·n and analogous S_GHG, scaled by world fleet size n≈1.15 billion vehicles

Norton diffusion-substitution model — combines an external-influence (Bass innovation, exponential) ODE and an internal-influence (logistic/imitation) ODE into n(t)=p[M−N]+ (q/M)N[M−N], solved to closed form N(t)=M(1−e^−(p+q)t)/(1+(q/p)e^−(p+q)t), then extended to a two-product CV→EV substitution with a market-entry delay τ2; p,q fit by MATLAB curve-fitting on 1985–2008 China private-car data
Well-to-Wheel (WTW) energy-efficiency model — chains Well-to-Tank and Tank-to-Wheel efficiencies (CV: 85%×17.9%≈15.2%; EV: 42%×67%≈28.1%) to compare end-to-end energy efficiency
Saved-fuel / extra-coal accounting model — S_crude=c/r and E_extra=c·e/a to convert displaced gasoline into saved crude and extra electricity/coal at given EV penetration levels

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP, Saaty) — goal/criteria/alternatives hierarchy with pairwise judging matrices, λ_max eigenvalue extraction, consistency check (CI=(λ_max−n)/(n−1), CR=CI/RI<0.1), to output generation-mix weights w2≈(thermal .304, hydro .255, nuclear .256, others .185)
key_techniques: One-model-per-sub-question architecture: each of the four official tasks is answered by a distinct, named, textbook model rather than one monolithic model — economic (LCC), environmental (Gaussian Plume + scenarios), adoption (Norton), generation mix (AHP)
Concrete running case study (Toyota RAV4 vs. RAV4 EV) with a real parameter table, so every abstract formula gets plugged with sourced numbers

Composing two simple ODE diffusion models (external Bass + internal logistic) into one combined model, then generalizing it to a substitution problem with a time-delay τ2 — shows derivation, not just citation
Closed-form ODE solution plus inflection-point analysis (giving timing of the adoption growth peak ~2042) rather than pure numerical simulation
Parameter estimation by MATLAB curve-fitting of p,q on historical fleet data, making the forecast data-driven
AHP consistency testing (λ_max, CI, CR<0.1) used as an internal validation gate before trusting the derived weights
Normalization/weighting of heterogeneous emissions into AP vs GHG aggregates so different pollutants can be compared on one scale
Sensitivity analysis as a bridge from numbers to recommendations — the ±5% ranking directly motivates the 'lower acquisition/battery cost' advice to manufacturers
Per-model Strengths/Weaknesses subsections that pre-empt judge criticism and even propose the better method (Genetic Algorithm to estimate Norton coefficients)
why_it_won: "It demonstrates broad, judge-pleasing modeling literacy: rather than forcing one technique, the team mapped each of the four official deliverables to an appropriate, recognized model (LCC for economics, Gaussian Plume for pollution dispersion, Bass/Norton diffusion for adoption forecasting, AHP for the generation-mix recommendation). That spread is exactly what Problem C's interdisciplinary, multi-part prompt rewards. Each model is actually derived and then instantiated with sourced, real-world numbers (RAV4 vs RAV4 EV, China fleet history, GM emission report, US Census fleet counts), so the work reads as quantitative rather than hand-wavy. Crucially, the team closed the loop back to the prompt's literal asks — it produces concrete answers (LCC = $115,100 vs $87,728; WTW 28.1% vs 15.2%; ~4.18 billion tons crude saved by 2045; generation weights 30/26/26/18%) and turns its sensitivity/AHP results into explicit, actionable recommendations for governments and manufacturers. Self-critical Strengths/Weaknesses sections on every model signal modeling maturity and honesty that judges look for."
section_highlights: "Structure tracks the prompt: an Introduction that restates the four goals as bullets and a parallel 'our approach' bullet list mapping each goal to a named model, so a judge can immediately see all tasks are covered. A clean Contents/section hierarchy (Economic → Environmental → Health → Future-development forecast → Key factors → Energy/savings → Generation mix) with explicit Basic Assumptions up front. Strong, reusable section pattern: present model → derive equations → collect data in a labeled table → compute results in a results table → evaluate strengths/weaknesses. Heavy, consistent use of numbered equations, parameter-definition tables, and figures (MATLAB dispersion plots, scenario bar charts, the CV/EV crossover forecast curve, WTT/TTW flow diagrams, AHP hierarchy tree, generation-mix pie). The Key Factors section is split cleanly into 'For government' and 'For vehicle manufacturers,' directly serving the prompt's stakeholder question."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Numerical sloppiness undermines credibility: the LCC results table shows RAV4 maintenance 'o' = 422224 (an apparent typo/error vs the ~$8,922 EV figure), and 'saved crude' is quoted in billions of tons while the parallel 'coal used' figures are given in kg (905.61/1035/1164.3 kg) — unit/scale inconsistencies that a careful judge would flag

Gaussian Plume model is misapplied: a moving road vehicle is forced into a STILL ground-level POINT source with constant uniform wind; the paper even admits this in its weaknesses but still presents it as the air-pollution centerpiece. The equation also has typographic inconsistencies (σ_x vs σ_z) and the 'simulation' figures are essentially illustrative scatter/circle plots, not a rigorous concentration field

Emission table treats power-plant emissions as if produced per individual EV without clearly modeling grid emission intensity or transmission losses; the AP/GHG normalization weights (6.11/3.86/1) are asserted with little shown derivation

AHP pairwise judging matrices are filled with subjective expert-style scores with no stated sourcing or robustness check beyond the consistency ratio; w1 vector entries are not normalized to sum to 1 as printed

Norton forecast rests on China private-car data but conclusions are stated globally; coefficient fit quality (R²/residuals) is never reported, and the model is calibrated on CV-only history to predict an EV product that didn't yet exist
Health-impact section (a required deliverable) is thin and almost entirely qualitative — one short paragraph with no model — leaving one of the four asks weakly addressed
Pervasive English-language and grammar errors ('t conventional vehicle', 'edibility' for flexibility, 'We make the and get') that, while not fatal, reduce polish
reusable_patterns: Map-each-deliverable-to-a-named-model: explicitly bullet the problem's sub-tasks in the intro and pair each with a specific recognized model — a fast way to guarantee full-prompt coverage on multi-part interdisciplinary problems
Anchor abstract models in one concrete, sourced case study (here RAV4 vs RAV4 EV) plus a parameter table, so formulas are always instantiated with real numbers

LCC / total-cost-of-ownership template for any 'is technology X economically viable' question: acquisition + maintenance − salvage + externality cost, then rank cost drivers via ±5% sensitivity analysis

Bass/Norton diffusion-substitution template for any 'forecast adoption / when does new tech overtake incumbent' question: combine external (innovation) and internal (imitation) ODEs, solve to closed form, fit p,q on historical analog data, read off the inflection point for timing
AHP template for any 'recommend an optimal mix/allocation' question: goal–criteria–alternatives hierarchy, pairwise matrices, eigenvalue weights, and a CR<0.1 consistency gate as built-in validation
Scenario-bracketing (low/medium/high penetration, e.g. 20/50/80%) to bound an uncertain future quantity instead of committing to a single point estimate
Per-model Strengths/Weaknesses subsection (and naming the better method you'd use with more time, e.g. GA for coefficient estimation) to demonstrate self-awareness and pre-empt judge criticism
Sensitivity-analysis-to-recommendation pipeline: let the most sensitive parameter directly dictate the concrete advice given to each stakeholder
Stakeholder-split recommendations ('For government' / 'For manufacturers') to cleanly answer 'who should do what' prompts
